title: Inviscid scaling in the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation from functional renormalization group and direct numerical simulations
authors: Liubov Gosteva, Dipankar Roy, Nicolás Wschebor, Léonie Canet
abstract: We show that the one-dimensional Kuramoto-Sivashinsky (KS) equation features a scaling regime characterized by the dynamical exponent $z=1$ at intermediate scales between the large-scale Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) scaling with $z=3/2$ and the small-scale non-universal behavior. This scaling regime is intrinsic to the KS dynamics since it arises from the vanishing of the effective viscosity when evolving from its microscopic negative KS value, to its macroscopic effective positive KPZ value. This vanishing of the viscosity deeply imprints the behavior of correlations at intermediate scales, which exhibit a universal $z=1$ scaling. This behavior pertains to the inviscid-Burgers universality class, which corresponds to the zero-viscosity fixed point of the KPZ equation. We evidence and characterize this so-far-overlooked scaling regime using both functional renormalization group and direct numerical simulations.
summary: This paper identifies a new intermediate scaling regime in the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation characterized by an inviscid Burgers exponent of z=1. This regime emerges because the effective viscosity vanishes as the system flows from its microscopic unstable state to its macroscopic KPZ state. The findings are robustly supported by both functional renormalization group theory and large-scale numerical simulations.
main_problem: The identification and characterization of a previously overlooked intermediate scaling regime in the one-dimensional Kuramoto-Sivashinsky (KS) equation.
main_result: The discovery of an 'Inviscid Burgers' scaling regime with a dynamical exponent z=1, which exists at intermediate scales between small-scale non-universal behavior and large-scale KPZ scaling (z=3/2).
method: The study employs Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) using the Wetterich equation and Next-to-Leading-Order approximation, complemented by Direct Numerical Simulations (DNS) using a pseudo-spectral method.
model_or_system: The one-dimensional Kuramoto-Sivashinsky (KS) equation, which describes systems with negative viscosity and positive fourth-order dissipation, relevant to chemical turbulence, flame fronts, and surface roughening.
key_observables: Dynamical exponent (z), effective viscosity (nu_kappa), two-point correlation function C(t, p), and time-reversal symmetry emergence.
important_parameters: Negative microscopic viscosity (nu < 0), positive fourth-order term (tau > 0), and the transition from the IB regime (z=1) to the KPZ regime (z=3/2).
main_assumptions: The use of a Next-to-Leading-Order (NLO) approximation within the FRG framework and the introduction of a noise term to facilitate the study of scaling regimes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of FRG functions and the convergence to a fixed point; Figure 2 shows DNS correlation function data collapse; Figure 3 compares the Fourier transform of the correlation function between FRG and DNS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the KS equation and the problem of intermediate scaling, presents the FRG framework and results showing the vanishing effective viscosity, validates these findings with DNS, and concludes with the implications for universality classes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Evolution of the functions (a) ˆf ν κ and (b) ˆf D κ under the FRG flow, starting from the KS initial condition ˆf D Λ = 1, ˆf ν Λ = −1 + ˆp2 (black dashed lines) and reaching a fixed point when κ →0 (black solid lines correspond to κ/Λ = e−8). Inset of (b): Fixed point shape of ˆf ν κ(ˆω = 0, ˆp) (blue, dashed) and ˆf D κ (ˆω = 0, ˆp) (magenta, plain) recorded at κ/Λ = e−400. They are identical, which indicates that time-reversal symmetry emerges at large distances, and they identify with the KPZ fixed-point scaling function.

FIG. 2. Two-point correlation function C(t, p)/C(0, p) from DNS, plotted in logscales. The function C(t, p) is computed for times t = n/4 with n = 0, 1, 2, . . . from the simulations. The black line indicates the KPZ scaling z = 3/2, and the orange one the IB scaling z = 1. Inset: Extracted scaling functions, in the KPZ range (p from 0.02 to 0.1, circles), using the scaling variable x = pt2/3; in the IB range (p from 0.12 to 0.3, crosses), using the scaling variable x = pt. They are compared with the exact Prah¨ofer-Spohn scaling function for the KPZ fixed point (dash-dotted line), and with the exact asymptotic scaling function (7) from FRG for the IB fixed point (dashed line).

FIG. 4. (a) Inverse of the decorrelation frequencies ϖα(p) from FRG; (b) decorrelation times τα(p) from DNS (see text for definition). Both show the IB scaling z = 1 over a range of intermediate momenta.
FIG. 3. Correlation function C(ω, p)/C(0, p) (a) from FRG; (b) from numerics. The KPZ and IB scaling regimes are in- dicated by the black and orange guidelines.
